Jericho stands before Israel not as a military puzzle to be solved but as a test of faith in the midst of apparent absurdity. The city is sealed tight, its walls formidable, its defenders ready. Israel has no siege weapons, no battering rams, no strategy that any commander would recognize. Instead, God gives Joshua instructions that sound like a ritual, almost like mockery: march around the city. Blow horns. Shout. And the wall will fall. There is no rational connection between the action and the outcome. This is precisely the point. God is not asking Israel to trust in military genius or human strength. He is asking them to trust in His Word alone. When Joshua relays these instructions to the people, he does so with absolute conviction, as if God's promise settles the matter entirely. The people obey in silence for six days—not a word, not a battle cry, just the sound of feet and horns. They are learning what it means to let God fight while they simply follow.

The silence itself is significant. In a culture where warriors shouted to intimidate and encourage themselves, Israel is commanded to keep quiet. There is something profoundly humbling about that restraint, about marching toward an enemy without the usual human expressions of courage or defiance. The silence says: this is not about us. It is about God's power, God's timing, God's promise. On the seventh day, when the horns blast a long note, Joshua finally gives the command to shout—and only then does the wall collapse. The shout does not cause the collapse; the shout is the response to God's action. Israel cries out not in fear or with the thin hope that their voices might do something, but in celebration of what God has already done. They shout because the victory is His.

But the victory comes at a terrible cost, one that we cannot soften. Everything in Jericho is devoted to destruction—every living thing. The text does not look away from this reality. Men, women, children, animals, all are killed. Only Rahab and her family are spared, and their salvation depends entirely on her earlier faith, her choice to hide the spies and confess that the God of Israel is the true God. Her rescue is an act of grace, a reminder that even in judgment, God keeps His promises to those who trust Him. Yet for everyone else in Jericho, the destruction is total and final. This is not a narrative that allows us to remain comfortable. God's judgment is real, His commands are absolute, and His people must obey even when obedience requires them to participate in something that weighs heavily on the conscience.

The closing verses lift our eyes from the rubble of Jericho to see the larger purpose. Joshua's fame spreads throughout the land, and the Lord is with him. The walls fell not because of Israel's strength but because of God's faithfulness to His covenant and His promise to give the land to Abraham's descendants. Every subsequent battle, every future victory, rests on this foundation: the God who speaks keeps His Word, even when His Word defies human logic and even when obeying it demands everything we have. As you read this chapter, ask yourself what walls in your own life require you to abandon confidence in your own strategy and instead trust in God's Word, even when that Word makes no earthly sense. The God who brought down Jericho is not smaller or less faithful now. He calls you to the same kind of obedience, to the same kind of silence before His power, and to the same kind of trust that He will do what He has promised.
